news_summary: Scientists have made breakthroughs in treating stubborn blood pressure, understanding brain cells linked to fear and PTSD, detecting cancer early through gut signals, and protecting teeth with innovative artificial saliva.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont réalisé des avancées décisives dans le traitement de l'hypertension artérielle rebelle, la compréhension des cellules cérébrales liées à la peur et au syndrome de stress post-traumatique, le dépistage précoce du cancer grâce à des signaux intestinaux, ainsi que la protection des dents grâce à une salive artificielle innovante.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado avances en el tratamiento de la hipertensión resistente, en el estudio de las células cerebrales relacionadas con el miedo y el trastorno de estrés postraumático, en la detección precoz del cáncer a través de señales intestinales y en la protección de los dientes mediante una innovadora saliva artificial.

articles.title: Scientists reveal new blood pressure treatment that works when others fail
articles.summary: A new pill called baxdrostat is showing strong results in lowering dangerously high blood pressure in people who don’t respond to standard treatments. In a large global trial, patients saw their blood pressure drop by nearly 10 mmHg, a meaningful reduction that can significantly lower the risk of heart attack, stroke, and kidney disease. By targeting a hormone that causes the body to retain salt and water, this treatment could offer a much-needed solution for millions struggling with uncontrolled hypertension.
articles.content:
A new medication has been found to significantly reduce blood pressure in people whose levels remain dangerously high even after taking multiple existing drugs. The findings come from a Phase III clinical trial led by a professor at UCL.

High blood pressure (hypertension) affects about 1.3 billion people worldwide. In nearly half of these cases, the condition is either uncontrolled or does not respond well to treatment. This greatly increases the risk of heart attack, stroke, kidney disease, and early death. In the UK alone, around 14 million people are living with hypertension.

Large International Trial of Baxdrostat

The international BaxHTN trial, led by Professor Bryan Williams (UCL Institute of Cardiovascular Science) and funded by AstraZeneca, tested a new drug called baxdrostat, which is taken as a tablet. The study included nearly 800 patients across 214 clinics around the world.

The research was supported by the NIHR Biomedical Research Centre at UCLH.

The results were presented at the European Society of Cardiology (ESC) Congress 2025 in Madrid and were also published in the New England Journal of Medicine.

Significant Blood Pressure Reductions

After 12 weeks, patients taking baxdrostat (1 mg or 2 mg once daily in pill form) experienced an average drop in blood pressure of about 9 to 10 mmHg more than those taking a placebo. This level of reduction is considered large enough to lower the risk of cardiovascular events.

Around 40 percent of patients taking baxdrostat reached healthy blood pressure levels, compared with fewer than 20 percent in the placebo group.

Principal Investigator, Professor Williams, who is presenting the results at ESC, said: "Achieving a nearly 10 mmHg reduction in systolic blood pressure with baxdrostat in the BaxHTN Phase III trial is exciting, as this level of reduction is linked to substantially lower risk of heart attack, stroke, heart failure and kidney disease."

How Baxdrostat Targets a Key Hormone

Blood pressure is heavily influenced by a hormone called aldosterone, which helps regulate salt and water levels in the body.

In some individuals, the body produces too much aldosterone. This leads to excess salt and water retention, raising blood pressure and making it difficult to control.

Scientists have long tried to address this imbalance, but it has proven challenging.

Baxdrostat works by blocking the production of aldosterone, directly targeting a major cause of high blood pressure (hypertension).

A New Approach to Difficult Cases

Professor Williams, Chair of Medicine at UCL, said: "These findings are an important advance in treatment and in our understanding of the cause of difficult to control blood pressure.

"Around half of people treated for hypertension do not have it controlled, however this is a conservative estimate and the number is likely higher, especially as the target blood pressure we try to reach is now much lower than it was previously.[1]

"In patients with uncontrolled or resistant hypertension, the addition of baxdrostat 1mg or 2mg once daily to background antihypertensive therapy led to clinically meaningful reductions in systolic blood pressure, which persisted up to 32 weeks with no unanticipated safety findings.

"This suggests that aldosterone is playing an important role in causing difficult to control blood pressure in millions of patients and offers hope for more effective treatment in the future."

Rising Global Burden and Future Potential

In the past, higher rates of hypertension were mainly seen in wealthier Western countries. However, changing diets, including reduced salt intake in some regions, have shifted the global burden. Today, far more cases are found in Eastern and lower income countries. More than half of all people with hypertension live in Asia, including 226 million in China and 199 million in India.[2]

Professor Williams added: "The results suggest that this drug could potentially help up to half a billion people globally -- and as many as 10 million people in the UK alone, especially at the new target level for optimal blood pressure control."

Notes
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough with a new drug, baxdrostat, that effectively lowers blood pressure in patients unresponsive to existing treatments. This has broad global health implications given the large number of people affected by hypertension worldwide, and the treatment targets a key hormone causing difficult-to-control blood pressure. The study is well-detailed, based on a large international clinical trial, and presents a meaningful advancement in managing a major health risk factor for heart attack, stroke, and kidney disease.
articles.personality_title: New pill baxdrostat lowers high blood pressure when other treatments fail
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – High blood pressure, or hypertension, affects about 1.3 billion people worldwide. Many patients do not see improvements with current treatments, leaving them at higher risk for heart attacks, strokes, and kidney problems.

**What happened** – A global study tested a new pill called baxdrostat on nearly 800 patients whose blood pressure remained high despite other medicines. After 12 weeks, those taking baxdrostat saw their blood pressure drop by about 9 to 10 points more than those who took a placebo. About 40% of patients reached healthy blood pressure levels with baxdrostat.

**Impact** – This is important because baxdrostat works by blocking a hormone called aldosterone, which causes the body to keep extra salt and water, raising blood pressure. By targeting this hormone, the pill offers a new way to help millions of people whose blood pressure is hard to control.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to study baxdrostat to confirm long-term safety and effects. If approved widely, it could become a key treatment for people worldwide, including millions in countries like the UK, China, and India.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Baxdrostat is a promising new medicine that lowers blood pressure by blocking a hormone, helping patients who don’t respond to existing treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelle pilule baxdrostat réduit la tension artérielle quand les autres traitements échouent
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’hypertension artérielle touche environ 1,3 milliard de personnes dans le monde. Beaucoup ne voient pas d’amélioration avec les traitements actuels, ce qui augmente leur risque d’attaques cardiaques, d’accidents vasculaires cérébraux et de problèmes rénaux.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une étude mondiale a testé une nouvelle pilule appelée baxdrostat sur près de 800 patients dont la tension restait élevée malgré d’autres médicaments. Après 12 semaines, ceux prenant baxdrostat ont vu leur tension baisser d’environ 9 à 10 points de plus que ceux sous placebo. Environ 40 % des patients ont atteint des niveaux de tension sains.

**Impact** – C’est important car baxdrostat agit en bloquant une hormone appelée aldostérone, qui fait retenir au corps trop de sel et d’eau, ce qui augmente la tension. En ciblant cette hormone, la pilule offre une nouvelle solution pour des millions de personnes dont la tension est difficile à contrôler.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à étudier baxdrostat pour confirmer son efficacité et sa sécurité à long terme. S’il est largement approuvé, il pourrait devenir un traitement clé pour des millions de personnes dans des pays comme le Royaume-Uni, la Chine et l’Inde.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Baxdrostat est un nouveau médicament prometteur qui fait baisser la tension en bloquant une hormone, aidant les patients qui ne répondent pas aux traitements existants.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva píldora baxdrostat reduce la presión arterial cuando otros tratamientos fallan
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La presión arterial alta, o hipertensión, afecta a cerca de 1.300 millones de personas en todo el mundo. Muchas no mejoran con los tratamientos actuales, lo que aumenta el riesgo de ataques cardíacos, derrames cerebrales y problemas renales.

**Qué pasó** – Un estudio global probó una nueva píldora llamada baxdrostat en casi 800 pacientes cuya presión seguía alta a pesar de otros medicamentos. Después de 12 semanas, quienes tomaron baxdrostat vieron caer su presión en unos 9 a 10 puntos más que los que tomaron placebo. Cerca del 40 % alcanzó niveles saludables.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque baxdrostat bloquea una hormona llamada aldosterona, que hace que el cuerpo retenga sal y agua, elevando la presión. Al atacar esta hormona, la píldora ofrece una nueva forma de ayudar a millones con presión difícil de controlar.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando baxdrostat para confirmar su seguridad y beneficios a largo plazo. Si se aprueba ampliamente, podría ser un tratamiento clave para millones en países como Reino Unido, China e India.

**Resumen en una frase** – Baxdrostat es un medicamento prometedor que baja la presión arterial bloqueando una hormona, ayudando a pacientes que no responden a otros tratamientos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a stylized human heart gently encircled by a translucent, glowing tablet symbolizing the new medication, with delicate, flowing streams of water and salt crystals subtly balanced around it to represent aldosterone regulation and controlled blood pressure.

articles.title: These overlooked brain cells may control fear and PTSD
articles.summary: Astrocytes, once thought to be mere brain “support cells,” are now revealed to be key players in fear memory. Researchers found they actively help form, recall, and weaken fear responses by interacting with neurons in real time. Changing astrocyte activity directly altered how strong fear memories became. This breakthrough could lead to entirely new treatments for anxiety-related disorders.
articles.content:
Imagine a star-shaped cell in the brain, reaching out with long, thin extensions to surround nearby neurons. This cell is called an astrocyte. For years, scientists believed astrocytes mainly acted as caretakers, helping hold neurons together and keeping brain circuits running smoothly.

New research is now challenging that idea. These widely distributed "support cells" appear to be just as important as neurons when it comes to forming and controlling fear memories.

"Astrocytes are interwoven among neurons in the brain, and it seemed unlikely they were there just for housekeeping. We wanted to understand what they're actually doing -- and how they're shaping neural activity in the process," said Lindsay Halladay, assistant professor at the University of Arizona Department of Neuroscience and one of the study's senior authors.

Halladay's team worked with scientists from the National Institutes of Health on this multi-institutional project, led by Andrew Holmes and Olena Bukalo of the Laboratory of Behavioral and Genomic Neuroscience.

Astrocytes Help the Brain Learn and Let Go of Fear

The study, published in Nature, focused on the amygdala, a key region involved in processing fear. Researchers found that astrocytes in this area play a direct role in how the brain learns what to fear, retrieves those memories, and importantly, learns when those fears are no longer relevant.

"For the first time, we found that astrocytes encode and maintain neural fear signaling," Halladay said.

This discovery challenges long-standing views that placed neurons at the center of fear processing and suggests new ways to approach conditions like post-traumatic stress disorder.

Watching Fear Form in Real Time

To explore how fear memories develop, the team used a mouse model and closely tracked brain activity. With the help of fluorescent sensors, they were able to observe astrocytes as fear memories were created and later recalled.

Astrocyte activity increased during both learning and recall. When fear memories were gradually extinguished, activity in these cells declined. The researchers then altered the signals astrocytes send to nearby neurons. Strengthening those signals made fear memories more intense, while weakening them reduced the response.

These results show that astrocytes are not passive helpers. They actively shape how fear is stored and expressed in the brain.

Disrupting Astrocytes Alters Brain Circuits

Changing astrocyte activity also affected how neurons behaved. When astrocyte signaling was disrupted, neurons struggled to form the normal activity patterns linked to fear. This impaired their ability to send information about appropriate defensive responses to other parts of the brain.

The findings challenge the traditional neuron-focused view of fear, showing that neurons do not act alone in generating fear memories.

Beyond the Amygdala: A Broader Fear Network

The effects of astrocytes extended beyond the amygdala. Changes in their activity also influenced how fear-related signals reached the prefrontal cortex, a region involved in decision-making.

This suggests astrocytes help not only create fear memories but also guide how the brain uses those memories to choose appropriate reactions in threatening situations.

New Possibilities for Treating PTSD and Anxiety

Understanding the role of astrocytes could reshape how scientists approach disorders linked to persistent fear, including post-traumatic stress disorder, anxiety disorders and phobias.

If astrocytes help control whether fear memories are expressed or fade away, future treatments might target these cells alongside neurons to improve outcomes.

Expanding Research Across the Brain's Fear Circuitry

Halladay's next step is to investigate astrocytes throughout the broader network involved in fear. The amygdala works alongside other brain regions. The prefrontal cortex helps guide decisions in fearful situations, while deeper areas such as the periaqueductal gray in the midbrain control responses like freezing or fleeing.

Although the exact role of astrocytes in these regions is still unclear, researchers believe they are likely contributing there as well.

"Understanding that larger circuit could help answer a simple question of why someone with an anxiety disorder might exhibit inappropriate fear responses to something that isn't actually dangerous," Halladay said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough revealing that astrocytes actively control fear memory, challenging previous beliefs and opening new avenues for treating anxiety-related disorders such as PTSD. The research has broad implications for mental health, a major public concern, and provides detailed context on the discovery and its potential impact.
articles.personality_title: New brain cells found to control fear and PTSD memories
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists used to think astrocytes, star-shaped cells in the brain, only supported neurons by keeping them healthy and stable. Neurons were believed to be the main cells handling fear memories and reactions.

**What happened** – New research from the University of Arizona and the National Institutes of Health showed that astrocytes actually play an active role in creating, recalling, and weakening fear memories. Using mice, scientists watched astrocytes light up when fear memories formed and fade when those fears lessened. Changing astrocyte signals made fear memories stronger or weaker.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how we understand fear in the brain. Instead of neurons working alone, astrocytes help shape fear responses. This could lead to new treatments for anxiety disorders like PTSD by targeting these cells, offering fresh hope for people who struggle with lasting fear.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study astrocytes in other brain areas involved in fear and decision-making. This might explain why some people have fear reactions that don’t match real danger, improving future therapies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Astrocytes, once seen as helpers, actively control fear memories and could become key targets for treating PTSD and anxiety disorders.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles cellules cérébrales contrôlent la peur et les souvenirs du PTSD
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques pensaient que les astrocytes, des cellules en forme d’étoile dans le cerveau, soutenaient uniquement les neurones en les maintenant en bonne santé. Les neurones étaient considérés comme les principales cellules responsables des souvenirs et réactions de peur.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle recherche de l’Université d’Arizona et des Instituts nationaux de la santé a montré que les astrocytes jouent un rôle actif dans la création, le rappel et l’affaiblissement des souvenirs de peur. En utilisant des souris, les scientifiques ont observé les astrocytes s’activer lors de la formation de souvenirs de peur et s’éteindre lorsque ces peurs diminuaient. Modifier les signaux des astrocytes renforçait ou affaiblissait ces souvenirs.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre compréhension de la peur dans le cerveau. Au lieu de fonctionner seuls, les neurones sont aidés par les astrocytes qui influencent les réponses de peur. Cela pourrait ouvrir la voie à de nouveaux traitements pour les troubles anxieux comme le PTSD en ciblant ces cellules.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier les astrocytes dans d’autres zones du cerveau liées à la peur et à la prise de décision, ce qui pourrait expliquer pourquoi certaines personnes ont des réactions de peur inappropriées.

**Phrase clé** – Les astrocytes, autrefois vus comme des cellules de soutien, contrôlent activement les souvenirs de peur et pourraient devenir des cibles clés pour soigner le PTSD et l’anxiété.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas células cerebrales controlan los recuerdos de miedo y PTSD
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos pensaban que los astrocitos, células con forma de estrella en el cerebro, solo apoyaban a las neuronas manteniéndolas saludables. Se creía que las neuronas eran las principales responsables de los recuerdos y reacciones de miedo.

**Qué pasó** – Una nueva investigación de la Universidad de Arizona y los Institutos Nacionales de Salud mostró que los astrocitos juegan un papel activo en crear, recordar y debilitar los recuerdos de miedo. Usando ratones, los científicos observaron cómo los astrocitos se activaban al formarse los recuerdos de miedo y se apagaban cuando esos miedos disminuían. Cambiar las señales de los astrocitos hacía que los recuerdos fueran más fuertes o más débiles.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia la forma en que entendemos el miedo en el cerebro. En lugar de que las neuronas trabajen solas, los astrocitos ayudan a formar las respuestas al miedo. Esto podría llevar a nuevos tratamientos para trastornos de ansiedad como el PTSD al enfocarse en estas células.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar los astrocitos en otras áreas del cerebro involucradas en el miedo y la toma de decisiones, lo que podría explicar por qué algunas personas tienen reacciones de miedo que no coinciden con el peligro real.

**Frase clave** – Los astrocitos, antes vistos como células de apoyo, controlan activamente los recuerdos de miedo y podrían ser claves para tratar el PTSD y la ansiedad.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing star-shaped astrocyte with delicate, radiating arms gently enveloping and connecting with stylized neuron-like shapes inside a softly illuminated brain region, symbolizing the active support and control of fear memories, rendered in natural earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden gut signals that could detect cancer early
articles.summary: A new study reveals that gut bacteria and metabolites may hold the key to detecting serious digestive diseases earlier and more easily. Using AI, scientists found that biomarkers linked to one condition can often predict others, showing these diseases are more interconnected than previously thought. This cross-disease insight could lead to faster diagnoses without invasive procedures.
articles.content:
Scientists have identified a set of biological markers that could significantly improve how gastrointestinal diseases (GIDs) are detected and treated. These conditions include gastric cancer (GC), colorectal cancer (CRC), and inflammatory bowel disease (IBD).

Their findings show that specific gut bacteria and chemical compounds, known as metabolites, are closely linked to each disease. This raises the possibility of diagnosing these conditions earlier and with less invasive methods. Some of these markers may even signal risk across multiple diseases.

AI Reveals Shared Gut Biomarkers Across Diseases

To uncover these patterns, researchers used advanced machine learning and AI-based tools to analyze microbiome and metabolome data from patients with GC, CRC, and IBD. By comparing data across diseases, they discovered that models trained on one condition could often predict markers for another. For example, models based on GC data were able to identify IBD biomarkers, while CRC models could accurately predict GC-related markers.

The research was carried out by teams from the University of Birmingham Dubai (Part of Health Data Science MSc Programme), University of Birmingham, Birmingham, UK, and University Hospitals Birmingham NHS Foundation Trust. Their results were published in Journal of Translational Medicine.

Lead co-author Dr. Animesh Acharjee from the University of Birmingham explained: "Current diagnostic methods like endoscopy and biopsies are effective but can be invasive, expensive, and sometimes miss diseases at early stages.

"Our analysis offers a better understanding of the underlying mechanisms driving disease progression and identifies key biomarkers for targeted therapies. These biomarkers could help identify diseases earlier and more accurately, leading to better, more personalised treatment."

Disease-Specific and Overlapping Gut Signatures

The study also highlighted distinct microbial and metabolic patterns for each disease, along with important overlaps.

In GC, bacteria from the Firmicutes, Bacteroidetes, and Actinobacteria groups were commonly found. Researchers also observed changes in metabolites such as dihydrouracil and taurine. Some of these markers were also linked to IBD, pointing to shared biological features. However, while they were useful for identifying IBD, they were less effective for detecting CRC.

For CRC, key indicators included bacteria like Fusobacterium and Enterococcus, along with metabolites such as isoleucine and nicotinamide. Some of these also appeared in GC, suggesting that these diseases may share underlying biological pathways.

In IBD, bacteria from the Lachnospiraceae family played an important role, along with metabolites like urobilin and glycerate. Notably, some of these markers are also involved in cancer-related processes, reinforcing the idea that these conditions are interconnected.

Simulations Show Clear Differences Between Healthy and Diseased States

The team also simulated how gut microbes grow and how metabolites flow through biological systems. These simulations revealed clear metabolic differences between healthy individuals and those with disease, further supporting the role of these biomarkers in diagnosis.

"Our study's cross-disease analysis emphasised the potential of using microbial and metabolic biomarkers identified in one GID to predict another," added Dr. Acharjee. "This innovative approach could lead to the development of universal diagnostic tools to revolutionise the diagnosis and treatment of for multiple gastrointestinal conditions."

Toward Non-Invasive Tests and Personalized Treatments

Looking ahead, the researchers plan to explore how these findings can be applied in clinical settings. This includes developing non-invasive diagnostic tests and more targeted therapies based on the identified biomarkers.

They also intend to validate their models using larger and more diverse patient groups, as well as investigate whether these biomarkers could help predict additional related diseases in the future.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where AI and microbiome research have identified gut biomarkers that could enable earlier, less invasive detection of serious gastrointestinal diseases including cancers. This has broad implications for improving diagnosis and personalized treatment for large patient populations, fulfilling the criteria of positive real-world impact, significance, and substance.
articles.personality_title: AI uncovers gut signals that may detect digestive diseases early
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been searching for better ways to detect serious digestive diseases like stomach and colon cancer, and inflammatory bowel disease. Current tests can be invasive and sometimes miss early signs.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of Birmingham and other institutions used artificial intelligence (AI) to study data about gut bacteria and chemicals called metabolites from patients with these diseases. They found that certain markers in the gut can signal not just one disease but several, showing these illnesses are connected. For example, a marker linked to stomach cancer could also help predict inflammatory bowel disease.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it suggests we could diagnose these diseases earlier and with less invasive methods than current tests like biopsies. It also means treatments could be more personalized, targeting the specific biological changes in each patient’s gut.

**What’s next step** – The scientists plan to develop new, simpler tests based on these markers and try them out in larger groups of patients. They also want to see if these markers can help detect other related diseases.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using AI, scientists found shared gut markers that could help detect and treat digestive diseases earlier and less invasively.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’IA révèle des signaux intestinaux pour détecter tôt les maladies digestives
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent de meilleures façons de détecter des maladies digestives graves comme le cancer de l’estomac, du côlon, et la maladie inflammatoire de l’intestin. Les tests actuels sont souvent invasifs et peuvent rater les premiers signes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Birmingham et d’autres institutions ont utilisé l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour analyser des données sur les bactéries intestinales et des substances chimiques appelées métabolites chez des patients atteints de ces maladies. Ils ont découvert que certains marqueurs dans l’intestin peuvent indiquer plusieurs maladies, montrant que ces maladies sont liées. Par exemple, un marqueur lié au cancer de l’estomac peut aussi aider à prédire la maladie inflammatoire de l’intestin.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle suggère qu’on pourrait diagnostiquer ces maladies plus tôt et avec des méthodes moins invasives que les tests actuels comme les biopsies. Cela permettrait aussi de mieux adapter les traitements à chaque patient.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de développer de nouveaux tests plus simples basés sur ces marqueurs et de les tester sur un plus grand nombre de patients. Ils veulent aussi voir si ces marqueurs peuvent aider à détecter d’autres maladies similaires.

**Phrase clé** – Grâce à l’IA, des scientifiques ont trouvé des marqueurs intestinaux communs qui pourraient permettre de détecter et traiter plus tôt et plus facilement des maladies digestives.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA descubre señales intestinales para detectar temprano enfermedades digestivas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan mejores formas de detectar enfermedades digestivas graves como el cáncer de estómago, cáncer de colon y la enfermedad inflamatoria intestinal. Las pruebas actuales pueden ser invasivas y a veces no detectan los primeros síntomas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Birmingham y otras instituciones usaron inteligencia artificial (IA) para analizar datos sobre bacterias intestinales y sustancias químicas llamadas metabolitos en pacientes con estas enfermedades. Encontraron que ciertos marcadores en el intestino pueden indicar no solo una sino varias enfermedades, mostrando que están conectadas. Por ejemplo, un marcador relacionado con el cáncer de estómago también puede ayudar a predecir la enfermedad inflamatoria intestinal.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque sugiere que podríamos diagnosticar estas enfermedades más temprano y con métodos menos invasivos que las pruebas actuales como biopsias. También permitiría tratamientos más personalizados.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean crear pruebas más sencillas basadas en estos marcadores y probarlas en grupos más grandes de pacientes. También quieren ver si estos marcadores pueden ayudar a detectar otras enfermedades relacionadas.

**Frase clave** – Usando IA, científicos encontraron marcadores intestinales compartidos que podrían ayudar a detectar y tratar enfermedades digestivas de forma más temprana y menos invasiva.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an abstract human gut shaped like a glowing, intricate network of intertwined colorful bacteria and flowing chemical compounds, with subtle overlapping patterns symbolizing different diseases, illuminated softly from within to represent early detection and personalized healing.

articles.title: Artificial saliva made from sugarcane protein protects teeth from acid and decay
articles.summary: Scientists have created an artificial saliva using a sugarcane protein that can protect teeth and fight bacteria. The key ingredient, CANECPI-5, binds directly to enamel, forming a shield against acids that cause decay. Early tests show it works even better when paired with fluoride and xylitol, significantly reducing damage to teeth. The innovation could be especially life-changing for cancer patients who lose saliva production after treatment.
articles.content:
A new type of artificial saliva, delivered as a mouthwash and made using a lab-modified sugarcane protein called CANECPI-5, could help protect the teeth of patients with head and neck cancer. These patients often undergo radiotherapy near the mouth, which can damage salivary glands and reduce saliva production. Because saliva plays a key role in controlling bacteria and maintaining oral health, its loss can lead to serious dental problems.

Researchers at the Bauru School of Dentistry at the University of São Paulo (FOB-USP) in Brazil found that CANECPI-5 creates a protective "shield" over teeth. This layer helps defend enamel from acids found in beverages like juice and alcohol, as well as acids from the stomach. The findings were published in the Journal of Dentistry.

International Research Collaboration and Study Design

The research was carried out during the doctoral work of Natara Dias Gomes da Silva at FOB-USP. The project included collaboration with scientists from the Federal University of São Carlos (UFSCar) in Brazil, the University of California in San Francisco in the United States, and Yonsei University College of Dentistry in South Korea.

The study is part of the Thematic Project "Modulation of acquired pellicle to control dental mineral loss: unveiling mechanisms to make therapies possible," coordinated by Professor Marília Afonso Rabelo Buzalaf at FOB-USP.

"We tested the mouthwash developed with CANECPI-5 by applying this solution to small pieces of animal teeth once a day for one minute. Based on these results, we'll conduct further research so that we can think about applications of this product," adds Silva, the first author of the article.

How Artificial Saliva Strengthens Tooth Enamel

"This is the first product that uses the concept of acquired pellicle [a thin protective layer that quickly forms on the tooth surface] to treat xerostomia, which is the sensation of a dry mouth caused by a lack of saliva. We use substances that will reformulate the composition of the proteins that bind to the teeth," explains Buzalaf.

"We've developed a process in which CANECPI-5 binds directly to tooth enamel, helping to make teeth more resistant to the action of acids produced by bacteria," Silva points out.

The study showed that CANECPI-5 works best when combined with fluoride and xylitol. In testing, the artificial saliva spray reduced bacterial activity and slowed tooth demineralization -- the process by which teeth lose calcium and phosphate, making them more vulnerable to cavities.

A Potential Solution for Severe Cavities After Cancer Treatment

This development is especially important because there is currently no dedicated product available to treat the severe cavities that often occur after radiation therapy for head and neck cancer.

"Artificial saliva improves the sensation of dry mouth and sores. This helps with discomfort and also combats bacteria. In some cases, the use of this type of product is only for a short time. In others, it's permanent, because many individuals lose the ability to produce saliva," adds Buzalaf.

The CANECPI-5 protein has already been patented. The next step is to scale up production by partnering with companies interested in bringing the technology to market.

"We've already tested the solution as a mouthwash, gel, and orodispersible film, which is a type of plastic that's placed on the tongue and dissolves, releasing the protein. We've tested it in several vehicles and found that CANECPI-5 works very well in all of them. We'll continue testing other technologies within the Thematic Project to use not only this protein, but others as well," says Buzalaf.

The Discovery of CANECPI-5 From Sugarcane Research

According to Flávio Henrique Silva, a professor in the Department of Genetics and Evolution at UFSCar who helped develop CANECPI-5, the work stems from earlier research on cystatins (a family of proteins involved in various biological processes) conducted as part of the Sugarcane Genome Project (SUCEST, FAPESP).

"At that time, our group identified and produced, in a recombinant form in bacteria, the first cystatin from sugarcane. We named it CANECPI-1. We then identified and produced five other cane cystatins, including CANECPI-5, which had potent inhibitory activities against cysteine peptidases, which are its target enzymes. Throughout our work, we noticed that this protein bound strongly to smooth surfaces, such as the quartz cuvettes used in activity measurements. This led us to conduct tests in partnership with Professor Marília Buzalaf on the binding of the protein to tooth enamel."

Researchers say CANECPI-5 is especially promising because it both protects enamel and helps regulate oral bacteria, making it valuable for future dental treatments.

"CANECPI-5 has also been used in the work of other colleagues in the field of dentistry, particularly involving periodontitis. We also have a collaborative project with a colleague from the Federal University of Uberlândia, using subcutaneous sponge implants in mice, which has shown that it's capable of reducing inflammation and promoting angiogenesis [the formation of new blood vessels] and fibrinogenesis [the formation of fibrin, a protein essential for blood clotting], important processes in tissue repair, making it a candidate molecule for use in wound healing," Silva points out.

Next Steps for Artificial Saliva and Dental Applications

Within the Thematic Project, researchers will continue exploring how CANECPI-5 interacts with other compounds.

According to Buzalaf, one direction is to combine CANECPI-5 with a peptide derived from statherin, a protein found in saliva, to determine whether this hybrid could better protect teeth from acids originating in the stomach. Another goal is to investigate its potential role in preventing periodontal disease.

"Another aspect of the Thematic Project is to associate CANECPI-5 with vitamin E because this vitamin acts as a carrier, bringing the protein into contact with the tooth. We imagine that this could facilitate the application of the product directly by the patient at home," says the researcher.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough in dental health with the development of an artificial saliva using a sugarcane protein (CANECPI-5) that protects teeth from acid and decay. This innovation has broad implications, especially benefiting cancer patients who suffer from dry mouth due to radiotherapy, a condition that leads to severe dental problems. The research is well-detailed, involves international collaboration, and shows promising early results with potential for wide application and commercialization, representing a meaningful positive impact on public health.
articles.personality_title: New artificial saliva from sugarcane protein protects teeth from decay
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – People who undergo radiotherapy for head and neck cancer often lose the ability to produce saliva. Saliva is important because it helps protect teeth from acids and bacteria. Without enough saliva, these patients can develop serious dental problems.

**What happened** – Scientists in Brazil created an artificial saliva using a protein from sugarcane called CANECPI-5. This protein sticks to tooth enamel and forms a protective shield against acids that cause tooth decay. The artificial saliva was tested as a mouthwash and other forms, and it worked even better when combined with fluoride and xylitol, reducing damage to teeth.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because there is currently no special treatment for the severe cavities that cancer patients can get after radiotherapy. CANECPI-5 not only protects teeth but also helps control harmful bacteria in the mouth. It could improve dental health and comfort for many people who have dry mouth from illness or treatment.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to work with companies to produce this artificial saliva on a larger scale. They will also test new combinations with other proteins and vitamins to make the treatment even more effective and easy to use at home.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A sugarcane protein-based artificial saliva offers new protection against tooth decay, especially helping cancer patients with dry mouth after treatment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle salive artificielle à base de protéine de canne à sucre protège les dents de la carie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les personnes traitées par radiothérapie pour un cancer de la tête et du cou perdent souvent la capacité de produire de la salive. La salive est importante car elle protège les dents contre les acides et les bactéries. Sans suffisamment de salive, ces patients risquent de graves problèmes dentaires.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques au Brésil ont créé une salive artificielle utilisant une protéine de la canne à sucre appelée CANECPI-5. Cette protéine adhère à l’émail des dents et forme un bouclier protecteur contre les acides responsables des caries. La salive artificielle a été testée en bain de bouche et d’autres formes, et elle fonctionne encore mieux avec du fluorure et du xylitol, réduisant les dommages aux dents.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car il n’existe actuellement aucun traitement spécifique pour les caries sévères chez les patients cancéreux après radiothérapie. CANECPI-5 protège non seulement les dents mais aide aussi à contrôler les bactéries nocives dans la bouche. Cela pourrait améliorer la santé dentaire et le confort de nombreuses personnes souffrant de bouche sèche.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de collaborer avec des entreprises pour produire cette salive artificielle à plus grande échelle. Ils testeront aussi de nouvelles combinaisons avec d’autres protéines et vitamines pour rendre le traitement encore plus efficace et facile à utiliser à domicile.

**Phrase clé** – Une salive artificielle à base de protéine de canne à sucre offre une nouvelle protection contre la carie dentaire, aidant particulièrement les patients atteints de cancer souffrant de sécheresse buccale après traitement.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva saliva artificial con proteína de caña de azúcar protege los dientes de la caries
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las personas que reciben radioterapia por cáncer de cabeza y cuello a menudo pierden la capacidad de producir saliva. La saliva es importante porque protege los dientes de los ácidos y las bacterias. Sin suficiente saliva, estos pacientes pueden tener graves problemas dentales.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos en Brasil crearon una saliva artificial usando una proteína de la caña de azúcar llamada CANECPI-5. Esta proteína se adhiere al esmalte dental y forma una capa protectora contra los ácidos que causan caries. La saliva artificial se probó como enjuague bucal y en otras formas, y funcionó mejor cuando se combinó con flúor y xilitol, reduciendo el daño a los dientes.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque actualmente no hay tratamientos específicos para las caries severas que sufren los pacientes con cáncer después de la radioterapia. CANECPI-5 no solo protege los dientes, sino que también ayuda a controlar las bacterias dañinas en la boca. Podría mejorar la salud dental y la comodidad de muchas personas con boca seca.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean trabajar con empresas para producir esta saliva artificial a mayor escala. También probarán nuevas combinaciones con otras proteínas y vitaminas para hacer el tratamiento más efectivo y fácil de usar en casa.

**Frase clave** – Una saliva artificial basada en proteína de caña de azúcar ofrece nueva protección contra la caries dental, ayudando especialmente a pacientes con cáncer que sufren boca seca después del tratamiento.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing sugarcane stalk gently wrapping a translucent, protective shield around a stylized tooth, with soft waves of clear mouthwash flowing around them, set against a simple, natural background of soft beige and muted greens.
